evidence_quotes.quote: Most of today’s games (even if they are one-person and non-online multiplayers) require a connection to the internet for something, so they can be ‘deactivated’ remotely.
or when the video games purchased became unplayed because the developer/distributor wanted to push people to republish the game by simply stop running the existing games.

paragraphs.text: I would like to ask for an additional point on the protection of digital products (video games, films, books, etc.) to be added to the bill. In recent years, people have very often bought a digital product and subsequently ‘take away’ from them, but of course the money has not been returned. This has already happened in relation to films (e.g. when the film was only available on streaming platforms, from where it was subsequently removed from the offer, also inaccessible to those who had bought the film before it), books (e.g. when Amazon/Kindle played the same with e-books) or when the video games purchased became unplayed because the developer/distributor wanted to push people to republish the game by simply stop running the existing games. Most of today’s games (even if they are one-person and non-online multiplayers) require a connection to the internet for something, so they can be ‘deactivated’ remotely. This is an unacceptable behaviour by publishers/developers and should not be allowed to do. Let’s think if all the pages of our favourite book (what we have taken for several years and are relaxed on the booklet) could only be assembled and could not be opened again, just because the publisher wants to have a new and ‘right’ version of the book. This is practically happening with a large number of digital media, which is not only an unacceptable practice for consumers, but it also makes it very difficult and/or impossible to preserve the media.
